body_txt: For centuries, the conventional wisdom was that Christopher Columbus's 1492 expedition was the first time Europeans reached the Americas. The 900s-1400s Icelandic settlements in Greenland were well documented, but the only records of Norsemen going further west than that were some family sagas of dubious accuracy, that spoke of an ill-fated expedition to a place called "Vinland". Interest in pre-Columbian Norse exploration of America was renewed toward the end of the 19th century, coinciding with a wave of Scandinavian emigration to the US and Canada, but it was still considered highly speculative and lacking in evidence, and many of the "artifacts" of Viking exploration offered up in this period were of dubious authenticity (see Hoaxes below). Then in the 1960s, archaeologists found genuine Norse artifacts on the island of Newfoundland, Canada, that were dated to the early 11th century, predating Columbus by nearly 500 years, note The exact years of the Norse presence in North America are highly debated, but a 2021 study of the wood used at the Newfoundland site dated it specifically to 1021, a decade or two later than some of the earlier dating estimates. suggesting that The Vinland Sagas were based in some grain of truth after all. This prompted a new wave of fascination with the Old Norse seamen and speculation that they could have ventured further into North America. This speculation became fuel for a number of stories. It might be a case of Historical Fiction where characters are Vikings attempting to colonize America, it might be a modern setting where something (or someone) the Vikings left behind becomes relevant to the plot, or it could even be an Alternate History where the Vinland colony persisted to the present day. A related concept that is often tied to American Norsemen is the continued presence of Norse settlements in Greenland. In real life, a Greenland Norse society existed between the 10th and 14th centuries, but eventually collapsed during the Little Ice Age through a combination of worsening climate and emigration back to Iceland and continental Scandinavia — modern Danish claims to the area were established much later. In alternate history, it's not uncommon for the Greenlander Norse to survive and for a relic Nordic country to exist on the icy island alongside, or sometimes just instead of, one in America. Note that at the time of writing, there's no concrete evidence that Vikings set foot on the continent beyond Newfoundland. However, due to Creator Provincialism the United States of America are a popular setting for these stories.
